title: SEGMENT 12 — The HIV life cycle as one story

lines.text: SEGMENT 12 — The HIV life cycle as one story
Let me now do something I think is pedagogically valuable.
I want to walk through the entire HIV-1 life cycle from start to finish, in one continuous narrative, using everything we've assembled.
If you can tell this story end-to-end, you have the central content of this lecture.
Picture a free HIV-1 particle in the genital mucosa of a person who has just been exposed.
Roughly 100 nanometers across, enveloped, with about 14 Env spikes sticking out.
Inside, two copies of plus-sense RNA, coated by NC.
Inside that, a conical capsid containing the RNA, reverse transcriptase, integrase, protease, and a handful of accessory proteins.
The particle encounters a CD4-positive cell — likely a CCR5-expressing memory T cell, or perhaps a dendritic cell that has come to sample antigen at the mucosal surface.
The gp120 SU on the particle binds CD4.
CD4 binding triggers a conformational change in gp120 that exposes the coreceptor-binding site.
The coreceptor — CCR5 in early infection — binds.
That triggers the gp41 TM to spring open, eject its fusion peptide into the host membrane, and collapse back, fusing the viral envelope with the host membrane.
The conical capsid, with all its contents, is now in the cytoplasm.
The capsid traffics along microtubules toward the nucleus.
Along the way — actually now we think it's mostly inside or at the nuclear pore — reverse transcription proceeds.
The tRNA-Lys-3 primer that was already base-paired to the PBS gets extended.
Minus-strand strong-stop DNA is made.
First strand transfer to R at the other end.
Full-length minus-strand DNA elongation.
RNase H degrades the template.
PPT-primed plus-strand strong-stop.
Second strand transfer at PBS.
Bidirectional elongation.
The product is linear double-stranded DNA with LTRs at both ends.
Roughly twenty hours from entry, in a typical infected cell.
The capsid, partially disassembled, traverses the nuclear pore with help from CPSF6 and the nuclear import machinery.
The pre-integration complex finds host chromatin — preferentially in transcriptionally active gene bodies, biased by LEDGF/p75.
Integrase performs 3'-end processing on the viral DNA in the cytoplasm and strand transfer in the nucleus.
The viral DNA is now permanently inserted into the host chromosome.
The cell is now a carrier of an integrated HIV provirus.
It will be, for the rest of that cell's life.
Now two things can happen.

Either the cell is in an activated state — NF-κB nuclear, P-TEFb available — and transcription begins, or the cell is in a resting state with closed chromatin around the integration site and the provirus goes latent.
In the active scenario, RNA pol II initiates at the U3/R boundary, makes a short transcript, pauses at TAR.
Once Tat is available, P-TEFb is recruited, the pause is released, and full-length 9-kilobase transcripts are produced.
Some get fully multiply spliced — making Tat, Rev, Nef.
Rev accumulates.
Rev binds RRE on incompletely spliced transcripts and exports them via CRM1.
Now singly spliced transcripts make Env, Vif, Vpr, Vpu, and unspliced transcripts make Gag and Gag-Pol via the −1 frameshift.
Gag traffics to the plasma membrane via myristoyl-PIP2 interactions.
NC grabs the genomic RNA dimer.
Gag oligomerizes.
Env spikes accumulate at the same membrane domains.
The membrane bulges outward into a budding particle.
ESCRT is recruited via p6 late domains.
Membrane scission.
An immature particle is released.
The immature particle activates its protease through dimerization.
Protease cleaves Gag and Gag-Pol at the canonical sites.
CA reassembles into the conical capsid.
The particle matures.
It is now infectious.
It floats off to find its next CD4-positive cell.
That's the cycle.
About twenty-four to forty-eight hours from entry to release of new virions.
In an untreated HIV-infected person, roughly ten billion new virions are produced per day, and roughly ten billion CD4 T cells are infected and die per day.
The immune system fights this for years, slowly losing ground, until CD4 counts collapse and AIDS develops.
That's the disease.
That's also the target spectrum for therapy.
Every step we just walked through is a drug-development opportunity.
Let's go to the pharmacology.
